title: Chapter 46: Timeline Thirteen
content: Tracing the Dark Patriarch's frequency signature back to its origin required every resource the alliance possessed.

Lin Yue navigated the space between dimensions for three days, mapping the frequency trail that the hijacked gate had left in the multiversal substrate—a path of Abyss-tinted resonance that led away from the alliance's twelve connected timelines and into uncharted territory. The further she went, the stronger the signal became, and the more disturbed the dimensional topology grew.

"It's like a wound," she reported, her voice thin with exhaustion as she emerged from a navigation session. "The substrate around Timeline-Thirteen is inflamed—frequency patterns that should be smooth are jagged, disrupted by the Abyss energy they've been channeling. And the damage is spreading. Not quickly—but steadily. Like an infection that hasn't been treated."
"Can you map the timeline itself?"

"Not from the outside. Their dimensional membrane is... different. Thicker, for lack of a better word. The Abyss energy they've been channeling has reinforced the membrane instead of weakening it—which shouldn't be possible, but is. Their membrane is stronger than ours, but it's fundamentally altered. It channels Abyss energy instead of blocking it."
*Assessment?* Ren asked the System.

*The membrane reinforcement is consistent with the Dark Patriarch's claims of "evolution" rather than "destruction." If the Abyss's energy can be repurposed to strengthen the dimensional barrier, it implies a functional integration that our containment model didn't consider possible. This doesn't invalidate our approach—but it does suggest that the Dark Liang has discovered techniques that we haven't.*
*Techniques that use people as batteries for Abyss energy.*

*Techniques that *convert* people into channels for Abyss energy. The ethical implications are significant. The practical implications are more so—they may have capabilities we can't match with pure containment.*
"How do we get inside?" Ren asked Lin Yue.
"The Stone might be able to help. It can perceive T-13's dimensional architecture—all I need is enough detail to find a safe entry point."

The Foundation Stone had been growing more capable with each passing week—its developing consciousness bringing not just awareness but agency, the ability to direct its pre-dimensional perception toward specific targets. When Ren asked it to examine Timeline-Thirteen, the Stone's response was immediate and detailed.

**THE MEMBRANE IS REINFORCED WITH CONVERTED FREQUENCY. IT IS STRONG BUT NOT UNIFIED—THERE ARE SEAMS WHERE THE ORIGINAL ARCHITECTURE MEETS THE ABBYSS MODIFICATION. I CAN PERCEIVE SEVENTEEN SUCH SEAMS. THE LARGEST IS HERE.**

The Stone transmitted a dimensional coordinate—a specific seam in T-13's membrane where the containment architecture met the Abyss modification, creating a vulnerability that a skilled navigator could exploit.
"I can work with that," Lin Yue said, studying the coordinate. "But I'll need someone with me—someone who can handle whatever we find on the other side."
"I'll go," Ren said.
"You'll be recognized. The War God walking into a hostile timeline—"
"I'll be the distraction. While they're focused on me, you map their infrastructure. Their Foundation Stone, their gate network, their troop deployments—everything we need to plan a defense."
"Ren—" Xue began.
"I know. It's dangerous. But we can't defend against an enemy we don't understand. And the Dark Patriarch made it personal when he invaded *our* home."
Xue's jaw tightened—the Ice Queen wrestling with the wife. The wife lost.
"Bring him back," she told Lin Yue. "In one piece."
"I'll do my best."

The entry point was exactly where the Stone had indicated: a seam in T-13's dimensional membrane, barely perceptible, like the gap between two curtains that had been pulled too quickly. Lin Yue widened it with careful frequency manipulation—not enough to damage the membrane, just enough to slip through—and they entered Timeline-Thirteen.
The first thing Ren noticed was the light.

It wasn't Abyss-dark, as he'd expected—it was *too bright*, the harsh, over-saturated light of a world where dimensional energy was so abundant that it leaked into the visible spectrum. Everything glowed faintly—the buildings, the streets, the people—with a luminescence that his enhanced senses identified as Abyss resonance, converted and redirected into a form that was almost—but-not-quite containment frequency.

Shanghai—T-13's version—was a city transformed. The skyline was familiar but *enhanced*: buildings taller, more ornate, crowned with frequency architectures that pulsed with Abyss-derived power. The streets were clean, orderly, and patrolled by black-uniformed channelers whose frequency signatures blazed with the converted Liang bloodline resonance that the Dark Patriarch had demonstrated during the invasion.

And the people—ordinary citizens, going about their lives—moved through the city with the same luminescence that everything else carried, as if the Abyss energy had been integrated into their very biology.

"They're all enhanced," Lin Yue whispered, her dimensional perception mapping the frequency signatures of everyone they passed. "Every single person in this city is carrying Abyss-converted Liang bloodline frequency. Not as much as the channelers, but enough to alter their cellular structure, their neural pathways, their..."
"Their humanity?"
"I don't know. They look human. They act human. But their frequency is fundamentally *different* from ours—not Abyss, not containment, something in between."
*Analysis,* Ren said to the System.

*The frequency pattern is consistent with partial conversion—the Abyss's transformation applied at a sub-cellular level, enhancing physical and cognitive capabilities while preserving consciousness and personality. Based on the Dark Patriarch's claims, this is the "successful" transformation that he described: preserved self, enhanced form.*
*Are they still human?*

*Define "human." Biologically, they carry significant genetic modifications—Abyss-derived sequences that wouldn't appear in any natural genome. Psychologically, they appear to retain self-awareness, emotional responses, and rational decision-making. The philosophical question of whether genetic modification changes identity is beyond my analytical capabilities.*
*Helpful.*
*I'm an emergency salvation system, not a philosopher.*

They made their way toward the Liang estate—T-13's equivalent, which dominated the city's center like a cathedral of power, its frequency architecture the most elaborate Ren had ever seen. The estate wasn't just a family compound; it was a *broadcast tower*, channeling Abyss-converted bloodline frequency into the city's infrastructure, feeding the enhancement that every citizen carried.
"If we can disable this—" Lin Yue began.

"We're not here to disable anything. We're here to understand. If we sabotage their infrastructure, we prove their point—that we're the aggressors, they're the victims, and the only path to safety is through the Abyss."
"Then what are we here for?"
"To talk."
The Liang estate's security detected them within twenty minutes of their arrival—but not with hostility. With *curiosity*.

Four black-uniformed channelers surrounded them in a perimeter that was precise without being aggressive—containment tactics, not assault tactics. The channeler in charge was a woman in her thirties, her frequency signature blazing with Abyss-converted Liang bloodline resonance, her expression one of professional interest rather than alarm.
"War God Ren Zhou," she said, as if greeting an expected guest. "And the cartographer, Lin Yue. The Patriarch anticipated your visit."
"Did he?"
"He said you would come. He said you would need to see for yourself." She gestured toward the estate's main entrance. "Please. He's waiting."

The Liang estate of Timeline-Thirteen was a mirror of Timeline-1's—and a distortion. The same architecture, the same layout, but infused with Abyss-derived frequency that made every surface glow, every corridor pulse, every room feel *alive* in a way that was both beautiful and deeply unsettling.

The Dark Patriarch received them in the main hall—a space that, in their timeline, had hosted family celebrations and war councils and a hundred other moments of human drama. Here, it was a throne room, with the Dark Tianming seated at its center on a frequency-constructed dais that elevated him physically and dimensionally above his visitors.
"Welcome to my world," he said, and his smile was the same thin, confident expression he'd worn during the invasion. "I hope you find it... educational."
"I find it disturbing."

"Of course you do. It's different from what you know, and different is always disturbing at first. But spend some time here—talk to our people, see how they live, understand what we've built—and you may find that your discomfort is just the reaction of a mind encountering something it hasn't learned to appreciate."
"We didn't come to appreciate. We came to understand."

"Understanding *is* appreciation. You can't truly understand something you don't appreciate—even if you ultimately choose to reject it." He descended from the dais, his Abyss-converted frequency rippling with each step. "Come. Let me show you what the Liang family was always meant to be."
The tour lasted three hours.
Three hours of seeing a world that was simultaneously a nightmare and a miracle.

Medical facilities where Abyss-converted frequency healed injuries that containment-based medicine couldn't touch, regenerating tissue, reversing diseases, extending lifespans by decades. Schools where children learned to channel before they learned to write—not with the grueling discipline of traditional practitioner training, but through an instinctive, almost effortless integration that the Abyss conversion made possible. Public infrastructure powered by dimensional frequency—clean, abundant, and available to everyone, not just the bloodline elite.
And the people—happy. Genuinely, measurably happy, their frequency signatures carrying the unmistakable resonance of contentment, purpose, and security.

"You're seeing what I wanted you to see," the Dark Patriarch said, watching Ren's expression carefully. "A world where the Liang family's power isn't hoarded or hidden but *shared*. Where dimensional frequency isn't a weapon to be guarded but a resource to be distributed. Where the Abyss isn't a threat to be contained but a partner to be embraced."
"The people don't have a choice," Ren said. "They were converted without consent—the Abyss frequency was integrated into their biology before they could understand what was happening."

"The same way your containment-based society integrates its citizens into a system they didn't choose—born into political structures they didn't vote for, educated in systems they didn't design, regulated by laws they didn't write. The only difference is the mechanism; the result—people living within a system that shapes them—is the same."

"The difference is that our system preserves their freedom to change it. Your system *is* them—woven into their cells, their neurons, their thoughts. They can't opt out because opting out would mean tearing themselves apart."
"And if they don't want to opt out? If they prefer this—prefer the health, the strength, the connection, the *certainty*—to the fragile, uncertain freedom you're offering?"
Ren didn't have an answer. Not a clean one, anyway.
"Come," the Dark Patriarch said, leading him to a final room. "There's one more thing I want you to see."
The room was a nursery.

Six infants, lying in frequency-enhanced cradles, their tiny bodies already carrying the Abyss-converted Liang bloodline resonance that would shape their entire lives. Their frequency signatures were peaceful, content, and *growing*—developing at a rate that Ren's enhanced senses identified as approximately three times faster than a baseline human infant.

"My grandchildren," the Dark Patriarch said, his voice carrying a warmth that was jarring after the calculated charm of the tour. "The next generation of Liang channelers. Born into the transformation, never knowing the limitation and fear that your timeline still clings to. They'll never be sick, never be weak, never be alone. They'll grow up in a world that was *designed* for them—not by accident, not by tradition, but by conscious choice."
"You're using them as props."

"I'm showing you what we're fighting for. The same way you showed me what *you're* fighting for when you let the Stone awaken—when you chose to parent a god instead of weaponizing it. That was a conscious choice too, Ren Zhou. And it was the right one—for you. But it's not the only right choice. It's not the only way to build a future worth having."
The Dark Patriarch's eyes were earnest—genuinely, deeply earnest—the eyes of a man who believed with every fiber of his converted being that what he was doing was right.

"I'm not asking you to agree with me," he said. "I'm asking you to understand that we're not monsters. We're not the Abyss's slaves, any more than your Foundation Stone is its slave. We're *people*—Liang channelers, husbands and wives, parents and children—who made a different choice than you did. And we're willing to fight for that choice, the same way you'd fight for yours."
"Even if fighting means converting us by force?"

"I hope it won't come to that. But yes—even then. Because what we're offering isn't slavery. It's *evolution*. And evolution sometimes requires the extinction of forms that can't adapt." He paused. "I'm sorry. I know that sounds monstrous. But it's the truth, and I owe you the truth."

Ren looked at the infants—three times faster growth, never sick, never weak, never alone. A future designed for them by a grandfather who loved them in his way, even as that love was filtered through an Abyss conversion that had reshaped his values as thoroughly as it had reshaped his frequency.
*What do I say to a man who loves his grandchildren into a form they didn't choose?*
*What do I say to a man who's right about everything except the one thing that matters—that the choice, not the outcome, is what makes us human?*
He said nothing. He turned and walked out of the nursery, out of the estate, out of Timeline-Thirteen, carrying with him the weight of a truth he wasn't ready to face.
Sometimes the enemy wasn't wrong.
Sometimes the enemy was just... different.
And that was the most frightening thing of all.
